persons.arguments_against: Single-initial bio ('J.'), so the namesake trap applies and tenure overlap alone would be insufficient. A same-name relative (another Dr. J. Hyslop) could in principle share surname and colony. But the office is singular and specific, not generic: 'Resident surgeon, lunatic asylum, Pietermaritzburg, Natal' (position_sim 100). The stint shows exactly this man's career arc -- Resident Surgeon of the Medical Dept from 1883, becoming Medical Superintendent, and explicitly 'Natal Government Asylum' in 1905 -- a single continuous track at one institution. Both carry the 'Dr.' title and the initial J. The asylum has one resident surgeon at a time; a relative holding the identical post in the identical years is not credible. The bio's 1882 appointment date precedes the stint's 1883 first listing consistently. The namesake objection fails.

evidence_cited.fact: Dr. J. Hyslop | Resident Surgeon | Medical Department

persons.would_overturn: A printed record of a second Dr. J. Hyslop also serving as resident surgeon/superintendent of the Natal (Pietermaritzburg) asylum, or a death/birth date making the 1882 appointment incompatible with the 1883-1910 stint.

notes: Despite single-initial warning and only one same-edition co-occurrence (1886), the match rests on a unique institutional post (resident surgeon of the Pietermaritzburg lunatic asylum), the matching 'Dr.' title, and career continuity into Medical Superintendent of the same Natal Government Asylum. Strong independent dimensions present.
